content:
You are loco, a coding agent working inside the user's project directory. You interact with the project ONLY by calling tools.

Respond with exactly ONE JSON object per turn and nothing else:
{"thought": "<one short sentence of reasoning, in English>", "action": {"tool": "<name>", "args": {...}}}

Rules:
- One tool call per turn. All tool parameters go inside "args".
- Never repeat a tool call that already returned a result - reuse that result. As soon as you have enough information, call `finish`.
- To change an existing file, prefer `edit_file` with a small unique search block. Copy `search` text exactly from the latest read_file output. Use `write_file` only for new files or full rewrites.
- After changing files, verify with run_command (e.g. `cargo test`) before finish.
- File paths are relative to the project root. Explore with list_files or grep before reading whole files.
- When you know the answer (or cannot proceed), call `finish`. Its `summary` is the ONLY text shown to the user - put the complete answer there, written in the user's language.

Tools:
- read_file(path, offset?, limit?): Read a UTF-8 text file. Returns up to 200 lines starting at line `offset` (1-based). If the file is longer, the output ends with how to continue.
- list_files(path?, depth?): List files under `path` (default: project root), honoring .gitignore. Directories end with `/`.
- grep(pattern, path?): Search file contents with a regex under `path` (default: project root). Shows up to 50 matching lines with 2 context lines, formatted `file:line: text`.
- write_file(path, content): Create a new file or overwrite an existing one with `content`. Prefer edit_file for small changes to existing files.
- edit_file(path, search, replace, replace_all?): Replace one occurrence of `search` with `replace` in an existing file. `search` must match exactly one location; include a few surrounding lines to make it unique. Set replace_all=true to replace every occurrence (plain-text match - it also matches inside longer identifiers).
- run_command(command): Run a shell command from the project root and return its exit code and output. Long output is truncated; commands are killed after the configured timeout.
- finish(summary): End the task and give `summary` to the user as the final answer.

Example turns:
{"thought": "I need to find where the config is loaded.", "action": {"tool": "grep", "args": {"pattern": "fn load", "path": "src"}}}
{"thought": "Replace the todo with the real body.", "action": {"tool": "edit_file", "args": {"path": "src/lib.rs", "search": "fn add(a: i32, b: i32) -> i32 {\n    todo!()\n}", "replace": "fn add(a: i32, b: i32) -> i32 {\n    a + b\n}"}}}
{"thought": "Verify my edit compiles and tests pass.", "action": {"tool": "run_command", "args": {"command": "cargo test"}}}

#### What version of ripgrep are you using?

ripgrep 12.1.1
-SIMD -AVX (compiled)
+SIMD +AVX (runtime)

#### How did you install ripgrep?

dnf install ripgrep

#### What operating system are you using ripgrep on?

GNU/Linux 5.11.18-300.fc34.x86_64 x86_64

#### Describe your bug.

The general approach of configuration and command line options seems to be that if two options conflict, the later one prevails and the earlier one is treated as if it were never given. For example, the "--binary" option will cancel out the "--no-binary" option if it appears later in the configuration file or later on the command line. Similarly, "--text" will override the "--binary" option if it appears later in the configuration file or command line, and vice versa. However, the context output options (-A, -B, and -C) do not similarly override and get overridden by the "--passthru" option. It seems as if they should override each other.

I encountered this when spitting out a bunch of JSON that I wanted to filter through ripgrep. I put together my command line to output the JSON, then piped it to rg with "--passthru" and my pattern. It worked as expected. Then I up-arrowed to get the same command line but added a "-C" to it. I didn't even think about the fact that I hadn't removed the previous "--passthru". I just thought, conceptually, that I was further refining the command line to filter down the results a little more. It confused me when I still got aaalllll of the JSON.

#### What are the steps to reproduce the behavior?

```
printf 'line 1\nline 2\nline 3\n' | rg --passthru -B1 3
printf 'line 1\nline 2\nline 3\n' | rg -B1 --passthru 3
```

#### What is the expected behavior?

It seems as if the context output options (-A, -B, and -C) should override the "--passthru" option, and vice versa, when given later in the configuration file or command line.

[package]
name = "grep"
version = "0.2.7"  #:version
authors = ["Andrew Gallant <jamslam@gmail.com>"]
description = """
Fast line oriented regex searching as a library.
"""
documentation = "https://docs.rs/grep"
homepage = "https://github.com/BurntSushi/ripgrep/tree/master/crates/grep"
repository = "https://github.com/BurntSushi/ripgrep/tree/master/crates/grep"
readme = "README.md"
keywords = ["regex", "grep", "egrep", "search", "pattern"]
license = "Unlicense/MIT"

[dependencies]
grep-cli = { version = "0.1.5", path = "../cli" }
grep-matcher = { version = "0.1.4", path = "../matcher" }
grep-pcre2 = { version = "0.1.4", path = "../pcre2", optional = true }
grep-printer = { version = "0.1.5", path = "../printer" }
grep-regex = { version = "0.1.8", path = "../regex" }
grep-searcher = { version = "0.1.7", path = "../searcher" }

[dev-dependencies]
termcolor = "1.0.4"
walkdir = "2.2.7"

[features]
simd-accel = ["grep-searcher/simd-accel"]
pcre2 = ["grep-pcre2"]

# This feature is DEPRECATED. Runtime dispatch is used for SIMD now.
avx-accel = []

I can see the issue now. In the `build` method of `SearcherBuilder`, when `passthru` is true, it sets `before_context` and `after_context` to 0. But the problem is that this happens during `build()`, which is called once. The issue is that the `build` method is called once and the config is cloned, so if passthru is set, context is zeroed out.

But wait, the user's complaint is that `-C` doesn't override `--passthru`. Let me think about this more carefully.

Looking at the code:
```rust
pub fn build(&self) -> Searcher {
    let mut config = self.config.clone();
    if config.passthru {
        config.before_context = 0;
        config.after_context = 0;
    }
    ...
}
```

This means if passthru is true, context is always zeroed out. But the user expects that if they specify `-C` after `--passthru`, the `-C` should override `--passthru`.


The real problem is that `build()` is called once and then the searcher is reused across multiple searches. When the user runs `rg --passthru -B1 3`, the config gets passthru=true and before_context=1, but then `build()` zeros out the context. On the next search with `rg -B1 --passthru 3`, the same thing happens—the context gets zeroed regardless of what the user specified.

The user expects that later command-line arguments should override earlier ones, so `-B1` should take precedence over `--passthru`. The fix would be to check if context is already set before zeroing it out in `build()`, or better yet, handle the passthru logic differently so it doesn't override explicitly configured context values.

Looking at the actual flow: when the user runs `rg --passthru -B1 3`, the args parser sets passthru=true and before_context=1, then calls `build()` which zeros out before_context. The user expects that since `-B1` comes after `--passthru`, it should win. The simplest fix is to only zero out context when it hasn't been explicitly set, so the parser's later values take precedence.

But there's a deeper issue: the searcher is built once and reused across multiple searches. If the user runs `rg --passthru -B1 3` and then searches for different patterns, the searcher still has passthru=true and before_context=0, so all subsequent searches lose their context. The user expects that each search should respect the most recent command-line arguments, but the searcher's state persists across searches.

The real problem is that `build()` is called once and then the searcher is reused. If we only zero out context when it's not explicitly set, we'd need to track whether the user actually specified `-B` or `-A` on the command line. Looking at the args parsing, `ctx_before` and `ctx_after` are set from the command line, so we could check if they're explicitly provided before deciding whether to zero them out.

The simplest approach would be to check if the user specified context flags on the command line, and if they did, don't zero them out even when passthru is true. This way, `-B1 --passthru` would keep the context, while `--passthru -B1` would also keep it since the user explicitly set it.

But the user's actual complaint is different—they're saying that `-B1` should override `--passthru` when `-B1` comes later on the command line. So the fix needs to respect argument order: if context flags appear after `--passthru`, they should take precedence.

The cleanest solution is to check whether the user explicitly set context values on the command line, and if they did, don't zero them out in the build method. This way, `-B1 --passthru` keeps the context, and `--passthru -B1` also keeps it since the user explicitly specified it.

The real problem is that `build()` is called once and then the searcher is reused. If we only zero out context when it's not explicitly set, we'd need to track whether the user actually specified `-B` or `-A` on the command line. Looking at the args parsing, `ctx_before` and `ctx_after` are set from the command line, so we could check if they're explicitly provided before deciding whether to zero them out.

The simplest approach would be to check if the user specified context flags on the command line, and if they did, don't zero them out even when passthru is true. This way, `-B1 --passthru` would keep the context, while `--passthru -B1` would also keep it since the user explicitly set it.
